speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Gabbai, you were just telling us about the Kapos who were overseeing you. Tell us more about them. Who were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: The one that I know was-- one Kapo was Kojak. That was a very good one. You know, very helpful, you know the guy that, in the beginning, you know. Because if he wasn't nice enough, you know, you couldn't survive that either. But the overall Kapo of the Crematorium II, where I was working, was by the name of Kaminski. He was very tough, but also, inside, I think, of this man was something very, very good in it. And he knew about the-- we used to make a-- would like to make a revolution there, an uprising.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he-- the Germans, you know after-- after that, that the-- you know, you want me to talk to you about the uprising? Actually, I'd like to wait on that a little bit.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Well, Kaminski was one-- the overall Kapo. He was tough with us, but I think, his heart, and he was a very nice, nice man.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he a Polish man, Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Both Polish, Jewish men, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you communicate with him? What language did you speak?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know, just [LAUGHS] we try.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, where did you sleep while you were working in the crematorium?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we had an upstairs on the crematorium. Upstairs were nice, you know, bunkers to sleep. Wasn't bad for us, you know. We-- we had good clothes, you know. We-- our clothes weren't the one with the-- like the camp. We would take a jacket from the people that were coming, and we had a jacket and a shirt and--

speaker: interviewer
text: So these-- the problem with the Sonderkommando is not that, when we were there, the things that the-- the food or the clothing. It's the inside of your soul. This is-- this is where our problem was. You know, it's very difficult to survive in circumstances like that. To be able to come out-- we-- we knew that we are not going to survive. How did you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because every six months, the Sonderkommando was always killed, gassed, and new people come in. This is how we came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: From-- from people that were there. They know that, every six months, there were a selection, a selection going on. You know, always, always, there's a replenishing. You know? So we knew that it was impossible. We knew exactly, from A to Z, how the final solution of the Jewish people was meant. And I'm going to tell you now that, as far as I know, we are only three survivors today in-- two in the United States. I think four. Two in the United States, one in Rome, and one in-- I think, in Tel Aviv. And when we are gone, you know, I don't think anybody-- anybody can tell that. I know that somebody was there, and that's what happened. But I-- we are only eyewitness of the final solution. That's why it's very important. And that's why I went-- you know, a few weeks ago, I just came back from Poland, you know-- with the young people that are hungry of knowledge what was going on for the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to talk about that more later on. But right now, you-- you mentioned that you were able to take clothing when you needed something. Let's talk a little bit about that. What kind of things were you finding that the people brought in, and what was done with those things?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they-- the SS told us to search every-- everything that was coming in. So we had the facility to take anything and put it aside. You know, also, one of our members, a Greek guy by the name of Cohen, you know, that used to extract the gold teeth. And, you know, they-- when-- when it happened that we know we are not going to survive, and we wanted to do something-- an uprising, a revolution, you know-- that was some things we were sending-- the partisans were about 30 miles away from us. They were not too cooperative with us. But just we were sending money to them, and they were sending knives, dynamite to be able to-- to do something one day. And that's what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you able to send money to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Through parties. You know, there's always means. you know, people-- with money, you can do anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So going back to the things you found-- let's say, for instance, the teeth, the gold teeth that were taken out of the dead bodies-- what happened with those things?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the-- the Germans were every day taking them away, you know. You put it in a-- in a can, whatever it was, and they will take them. As a matter of fact, I was listening the other day that, in Switzerland, all this-- I don't know if you see last week-- all that gold belongs to the Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other kind of things did you find, besides, obviously, the gold teeth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they had money, dollars. A lot of people come with dollars. They had diamonds. Any-- you know, most of their possessions, they could put it in places, you know, that nobody would see it. You know, that--

speaker: interviewer
text: Like, what kind of places?

speaker: interviewee
text: Different places. Especially when they were-- after-- after they were dead. A lot of things were found, you know, in ears, in-- I can't even mention.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who would remove these things from those places?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- if we were around there, we would remove it. But we couldn't do nothing with it, because there's no place to do it, you know. They were going to the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were not working, and you were together with the other Sonderkommandos in your barracks, let's say, what kind of things did you talk about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Always you talk about the old things, about the nice things, to be free. To be able to do that, to be with your family. You know, we were young people. We were just in the stage of our lives, you know, that you need everything, you know. To be able to survive, and to have a nice life. That didn't happen. Most of the time, we were doing that. And that took most of our time, because we were working, especially the time I was there. There were a lot of work to be done. You know, it never stopped.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 22.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who were you with at-- during your work hours, and in your free time? Who did you spend your time with?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were together with my brother, and the two brothers of Venezia, and Danny, and Marcel Nadjary that-- he survived. He was the one that, I think, in 1980, they found a bottle in the crematoriums with a few notes of his. He used to keep notes. He didn't tell anyone. He has a book in Greek that came out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you mentioned that you knew what would happen to the Sonderkommando. Did you think about that often? Did you care that you were going to die?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure, sure. We cared [INAUDIBLE]. Life is too precious, you know. And we were too young to die. That's why we decide to make an uprising. And the uprising was initiated by-- there were 19 Russians, all from the army. Officers. And was one Greek captain by the name-- by the name of Baruch. That they put together the uprising. And actually, they put a date on the uprising, and the date that couldn't be kept was October 7, 1944. And the date couldn't be kept because, at that time, there were a lot of-- it happens that that day, a lot of Polish people came to the camp. I don't know why.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they postponed. So the next day-- the next day they postponed-- the SS, they-- they arrest Kaminski. And they took it in Crematorium IV, and they killed him there, with a lot of gypsies. Speaking about the gypsies, did you see the gypsies in the camp? Were you aware that they were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know if I had gypsies, but we knew that there were gypsies there, yeah. They were killing a lot of Polish, too, you know? But, you know, 99.9% were the Jewish people. Anyone that has a number is Jewish. The people that were not Jews, they didn't have no number.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the experimental block? Did you know what was going on there? Did you see it ever?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know. I didn't know, but I know that Mengele was doing experiments, you know, in Block 10, in Auschwitz. I think there is a lady here, living here-- I don't know if you-- you interview her? No? But that's the name-- by the name of [? Taboch ?] that I think was on that block.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you always working in the same crematorium?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, always I work in Crematorium II.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the other crematorium, in relation to where you were? Can you give us an idea of where they were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, there were one, two, three, four across. Across. Not too far away. No. You could see everything that was happening.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when they started bringing in all of these Hungarian transports, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was there more involvement with the SS? Did you see more SS taking part, perhaps, in the killings, or shootings, or-- or gassings?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but you know, always when there were-- if it wasn't enough of a transport, there always was 10, 20, 30 people. We-- they were coming, we had to take them by the ear, and wait in there, and behind the German will kill them. Go down and clean up the-- and going to the ovens. If there were-- if there no were-- when there were 1,500 or 2,000, then they put it in the gas chambers, but for a small amount, you know, they used to shoot it by individual.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when the SS would shoot these people individually, where was this done?

speaker: interviewee
text: Done in front of us, in the crematorium.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you mentioned that you--

speaker: interviewee
text: You have to pick it up by the ear. Bring them that and keep them by the ear, and the SS would be behind, shooting them. You know, they would die immediately. Would go down, and somebody would pick it up, and then we had to clean up the blood. Things that I-- I don't know, I just-- it's very hard to say it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think the purpose was for holding them by the ear? What was the reasoning for that, do you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know, to stand up. I don't-- to not to move.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was doing the shooting at times like this?

speaker: interviewee
text: SS.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know any of these SS by name?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were, yeah. I don't remember their names. But there-- one was a Holland guy, from Holland, SS. He wasn't doing that. But there was another one. I don't remember his name. He was a tall, red-faced man that-- he was doing most of the shootings. Tall, very tall.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did these SS react to you? How did they treat you or behave towards you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were OK with us, you know. We didn't-- we never-- we didn't have too much to do with them, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were working in the crematorium, did you have a chance to ever see any of the important people of the camp come through, or any high ranking SS?

speaker: interviewee
text: They used to come, you know, after there were-- there were-- before we open up the gas chamber, they used to come frequently from Berlin. I don't know their names. They must be people from Berlin seeing how the Jewish people were dying.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- when these SS officers would come to watch, what did you see? How did they act? What did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Most of the time, they were happy people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it both men and women that would come?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the women, were they also SS?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There were plenty of SS women, you know, around the crematorium there, you know? Especially on the-- when the bunkers were in use, you know. There were a lot of women around there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when you say the bunkers were in use, what exactly do you mean by that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when there weren't enough transports, that the gas chambers couldn't hold it, you know, then they had to, you know-- after the gas chamber, the crematoriums couldn't hold thousands of people. You know, there were-- at one point, there were coming there, what, 5,000 6,000, 7,000, 10,000 a day.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they couldn't hold it, the crematorium. So they had to, because was easier to-- to send-- put it in the bunker, and burn them in the bunker. They used to put some-- a lot of foliage there, and put some gas, and then threw the corpse after the gas. Now, when you say bunker, are you referring to like a big pit in the ground?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who would make sure that these fires kept burning? Who would put the bodies in these big pits?

speaker: interviewee
text: Crematorium people would put the bodies.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long would these keep burning before the bodies were actually destroyed?

speaker: interviewee
text: It takes-- it takes hours, you know, for 2,000 people to be burned.

speaker: interviewer
text: In these pits, the bodies that were burning, were they bodies that had already been gassed in the gas chamber?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Oh, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any shootings that took place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably there were. I don't-- I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in this time, from April of 1944 until you left Auschwitz, the months that you worked there, how did you come to be part of the plan for this uprising that you mentioned earlier?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were told by-- by the people that put the uprising together that there will be an uprising, because we had to take part of it. Not only Crematorium II, but the III and IV together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in contact with the other crematoriums?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, we were in contact. But the-- what I remember is that the-- the-- we couldn't keep that October 7 date, because of that. So then, Kominski was killed, you know. And then, the next-- after a few days, they-- they selected 200 from the crematorium-- from all the crematorium, 200 people-- and they-- they gassed them, and killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who selected?

speaker: interviewee
text: The SS selects. You know, always they were with names. They come with the numbers, you know, and they selected. And they-- they were-- time, you know, at that point, I-- if I remember correctly, it's just-- there were, in October, there were-- the transports were easing up. There weren't enough transports.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they, after they selected the 200, they went to Crematorium IV, and they selected another 200. So at that time, I-- I-- I remember was we saw-- it was early in the morning when-- when we saw some fires on Crematorium-- on Crematorium III. So we went upstairs to see what was going on, and then the SS told us to come down immediately, and this-- and there was the-- the uprising. No, we were-- the Crematorium II-- we were ready to-- to put fire and get killed with the crematorium.

speaker: interviewee
text: But there were the Polish people, at that time in the Crematorium II, didn't want us to do it, because this would be for no avail. So we didn't did nothing. So the hundred of us were taken to a room, and then Commandant Kramer came in, and registered all our numbers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we talk about that, let me ask you. When the uprising was being planned, who exactly was doing the planning, and how did they get gun powder, or guns?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were-- I don't know exactly, but the 19-- the 19 Russians, and-- and the Greek guy, you know, they got together, and with the help of sending gold, or sending money there, we used to get to all this from, I think, a manufacturer around-- around there called Union. U-N- I- O-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you given any guns, or gun powder?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were told to-- to get dressed well, you know? That-- that's the only thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your job supposed to be in the uprising? What exactly were you supposed to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we supposed-- we supposed to-- to cut the tires of anything that was from the SS. And also, if we could kill the SS. There was one SS in Crematorium II.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you saw the burning in Crematorium III, what happened exactly? Were you able to do anything at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. Could you [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewer
text: It's all right.

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened is that we went upstairs in our rooms to get better view of what was happening. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: I need you to sit back a little bit so we can hear you better. So when you went upstairs to see the fire, do you remember being upstairs and watching what was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were watching, but we couldn't see anything. Also, you know, Crematorium I was-- was calling us. So I don't remember exactly what happened, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you say Crematorium I was calling you, what do you-- how exactly--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they see-- when they see the fires, you know, they thought that-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in Crematorium I, they thought that the Germans were killing all of us. So what they did, you know, they-- one of the Greeks in Crematorium I says, let's go. Let's start the uprising. And-- but-- and they had also the dynamite, and they didn't put the dynamite. So what happened is the Germans, the SS came in, and they kill everybody in Crematorium I. And what happened also is that, you know, the next day, they brought all the corpses of the Crematorium I, and we had to put them, you know, in the ovens.

speaker: interviewer
text: When they brought these other Sonderkommandos from Crematorium I to you, had they all been shot?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you have to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing, just burn them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So while Crematorium III was burning, were you surrounded by SS?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we were surrounded by SS. And-- and also, you know, in-- in Crematorium-- Crematorium III, you know? Also that was also the-- they went and they-- this SS came in, and they start shooting, on site, everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the time when Crema-- Crematorium III was burning, did you know how it happened? Or did you find out how it was burning, how it started, what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they start shooting. I-- we don't know what-- how it happens. That's why didn't it succeed, the revolution. We were-- we weren't coordinated enough to be able to do it. And then, as I remember, they were telling me that the-- the partisans, they weren't cooperative enough for us, the Jews, to do the job.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you were gathered together in this room, all of you, and earlier you said that Commandant Kramer--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Kramer.

speaker: interviewer
text: --came in, what exactly did he say to you? Did you know what was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. They just-- just-- you know, I thought that we were going to get killed. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they make you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Just, we didn't do anything. Then-- then that happened in Crematorium I, you know, and then everybody was killed. But after that, what was happening is that the they-- they-- the transports stopped. [INAUDIBLE] You know, they came from-- they selected another 200. And then they came from Berlin. Came-- you know, they-- they came in from Berlin and they say to stop-- stop killing. Stop killing, and the transports stopped. This is-- this is how everything started. Now when we were-- when that happens, we were-- the Sonderkommando, whatever were left of us-- I think 200 of us were left-- we were just cleaning the burned Crematorium III.

speaker: interviewer
text: That-- when you say cleaning burned--

speaker: interviewee
text: The burned crematorium, yeah. We were cleaning it out. You know, it was all burned up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you interrogated at all? Did they ask you questions, as who was in charge, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nothing, nothing. Because most-- most of the people, a lot of the people of the Crematorium I and III, you know, they start going in the fields. This is where the SS just kill everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened after they came and they stopped the transports? What were you doing then?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were just cleaning the Crematorium I-- I and-- no, Crematorium III. And-- and then, we-- on January 15, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the-- the Germans were uneasy because the Russians were advancing from Krakow to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what they did, they-- they called 100 more from the-- to be-- from the Sonderkommando, and they send them in another-- they say that they were sending them to another camp. And in them were all Polish, and Kojak was there. So-- but we knew better, you know? They were killed right away. The other day, they were killed. So-- You said-- excuse me-- but you said Kojak was there. Tell us again who Kojak was.

speaker: interviewee
text: Kojak was one of the Kapos of Crematorium II. So we were only left, now, 100 of us, and-- and 26 Greeks. And January-- January 15, when the Russians were coming, the-- on January 18, the Birkenau-- Auschwitz and Birkenau-- the Birkenau actually was evacuated. And we were on that Block 13. And the Germans were mixed up. They didn't know what they were doing, so we came out of that Block 13, and we got together with all the camp, the thousands of people, and start marching. They put us to march from Birkenau to Auschwitz. That took quite a few hours, about three miles, two and a half miles.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who were you with at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know who I was with. I was 20-20 marching. I was alone. 20-20 marching. I think my brother and Danny was together, but I was with other people, 20-20 marching. We march to Auschwitz, and from there, after stopping a few hours, we start walking for days. Two, three days walking. And I remember then very perfectly was the-- the 18th of January, 1945, and outside was 23 below zero. And the only thing that I can tell you today is that I survived by closing my eyes and saying, how beautiful Athens with the sunshine. And repeating that, many times, I start sweating. Believe me, I am telling you now. I had a blanket, and I threw it out. And we marched, and we marched for days until we arrive to a railroad station, where they put us in open wagons, there-- 150 in each one. And we-- after a day or so, we arrive at Mauthausen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you arrived to Mauthausen, and while you were on this march with all these people, can you give us an idea of about how many people there were marching all together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Marching thousands of people. And whoever couldn't march, the-- the army, the SS, was behind us. And they would kill anyone that they couldn't make it. So we arrive much, much less in our destination of Mauthausen.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you got to these trains, had you been given any food during any of this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nothing. When we left Auschwitz, we had, I think, 150 grams of bread and some margarine. That's the only thing. I remember that I was walking in Linz, and was raining. Was raining, and I-- I got down, and I put mud in my-- I was eating mud. Mud.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to stop right now and change the tape, and we'll go on.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
